clusters.summary: Children connect spoken Japanese to written forms, learn hiragana and katakana conventions, and begin handling text direction, sound marks, small kana, and long-vowel spelling.
Children develop legible handwritten Japanese, first through kana formation and later through manuscript-paper layout used for school writing.
Children begin noticing written sentence boundaries and basic punctuation as explicit features of school Japanese.

Lower-elementary kanji routines now develop meaning links, stroke-count checking, okurigana boundaries, compound labels, component position, on/kun verification, jukugo relation labeling, and lookup reflection.
Children expand vocabulary by noticing how known kanji combine into compounds and how word parts carry meaning across school texts.
Children start using dictionaries and reference tools to resolve unknown words and support independent reading.
Lower-elementary literary reading now adds who/why story questions, speech-bubble voice, feeling-evidence pairs, poem image sketches, and theme trackers across chapters.
Children learn to find central information in explanatory texts and use headings, captions, diagrams, and tables as part of comprehension.
Children progress from complete sentences to ordered paragraphs and then to opinions supported by reasons.
Children learn to listen for important details in classroom explanations, peer comments, and short stories.
Children develop classroom interaction by asking relevant questions, taking connected turns, and presenting short reports clearly.
Children begin monitoring their own comprehension and selecting repair strategies such as rereading, asking, or looking up a word.
Children notice that Japanese changes by situation, region, and expression type, including polite classroom language, dialect, proverbs, idioms, and set expressions.

Secondary informational reading develops from source summary and visual-prose integration to media framing, public statement rhetoric, academic abstract moves, and multi-source public controversy analysis.
Older students connect modern Japanese with classical language, kanbun, literary culture, and historical changes in expression.
Secondary writing develops from arguments with counterpoints to research synthesis that evaluates and integrates multiple sources.
Advanced literary reading develops critical-lens evidence grids, narrative time and symbol systems, literary-cultural source positioning, and reinterpretation of canonical readings.
Upper-elementary informational reading shifts from finding information to comparing source claims, selecting evidence, and preparing for inquiry.

Upper-elementary writing now deepens report section planning, evidence-limited summaries, argument paragraph order, source synthesis, counterclaim response, intro-conclusion alignment, and cohesion mapping.
Secondary language awareness includes sentence constituent analysis and high-school attention to definitions, distinctions, and logical wording.
Older students ask evidence-focused questions, synthesize viewpoints, present inquiry findings, and defend public-issue arguments with sources.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Oral Language & Listening now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Learning Strategies & Reflection now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Kanji literacy grows through grouped grade allocations, components, readings, compound structures, Joyo reference use, public-text register, and classical or kanbun notation rather than one node per character.

Vocabulary work develops from semantic grouping and precise word choice to academic compounds, domain-specific research terms, rhetorical vocabulary, critical terminology, and disciplinary lexicon synthesis.
Elementary grammar knowledge grows from sentence boundaries, particles, and word classes into modifier scope, clause relations, passive perspective, and sentence-complexity control.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Handwriting & Transcription now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.
Secondary grammar awareness develops from cohesion, nominalization, and modality to audience-register choices, definition boundaries, and metadiscourse for extended argument.
Language-culture study connects keigo, spoken/written style, classical inflection, and kanbun readback to modern expression and interpretation.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Reading Literature now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Informational reading develops from locating answers and cause-effect relations to integrating diagrams, comparing viewpoints, analyzing argument warrants, and reading contemporary issues through multiple lenses.
Source work develops from simple note cards to quotation, paraphrase, discussion summaries, source triangulation, annotated bibliographies, and comparative synthesis matrices.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Writing Composition now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.
Elementary discussion and presentation develops from pair talk and show-and-tell into role-based group discussion, interviews, source-supported presentation, debate listening, and consensus building.
Secondary discussion develops from follow-up question chains and facilitator summaries to debate flow notes, roundtable role rotation, colloquium question design, and symposium moderator synthesis.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Reading Informational Texts now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Information Use & Media Literacy now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Vocabulary & Word Knowledge now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Kana & Orthography now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Kanji literacy develops through mixed-script boundaries, okurigana, multiple readings, radical lookup records, homophone selection, compact academic notation, font variation, and kanbun reading supports.

Written grammar awareness includes punctuation as sentence structure, quotation and comma copyediting, paragraph-flow proofreading, secondary notation conventions, and punctuation scope in logical prose.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Language Culture & Variation now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.
Secondary language culture connects classical rhythm, kobun function words, honorific effects, public-discourse quotations, kanbun paraphrase, and language identity across classical and modern texts.
Secondary reflection moves from revision logs and source-decision logs to argument conference reflection, source-trail reflection, interpretive-assumption review, and final portfolio transfer claims.
Elementary orthography develops from kana chunking, particle boundaries, copying accuracy, and punctuation toward audience-aware mixed-script review in public writing.

Elementary kanji learning is grouped by meaningful school capabilities: calendar and number kanji, semantic fields, component analogy, compound relations, academic jukugo, and notation choices for audience.
Elementary literary reading grows from action and feeling words to direct speech, motive, viewpoint shifts, quotation commentary, and comparing style across works.
Elementary information use develops from labels, tables, and source note cards to visual credits, evidence relevance, multi-source synthesis, and publication proofreading.
Elementary composition develops from copying and adding details to paragraph focus, report alignment, criteria-based revision, and public presentation choices grounded in evidence.
The upper-elementary to high-school bridge treats kanji as morphemes, compounds, notation choices, and classical/kanbun reading supports rather than isolated character items.
Informational reading bridges from comparing text structures and source conflicts to media framing, rebuttal structure, policy counterevidence, and public source authority.
Discussion develops from source-supported turns to evidence protocols, facilitator return-to-evidence moves, debate cross-examination, and consensus work on contemporary issues.
Language culture bridges polite requests, classical quotation glossing, kobun and kanbun paraphrase, and historical register change in contemporary public discourse.
Reflection bridges evidence-gap review, strategy transfer into lower secondary, stance separation, debate bias, high-school language goals, and inquiry method limitations.
Advanced high-school grammar awareness focuses on definition testing, hedging by evidence strength, metadiscourse, and source voice control in extended logical arguments.
Advanced language culture connects classical function words, kanbun paraphrase justification, modern reception of classical expressions, and public language identity case studies.
Advanced information use moves from scholarly source-position maps and digital annotation layers to public controversy source-role matrices and final bibliography integrity.
Advanced oral work develops colloquium counterquestions, limitation responses in oral defense, symposium question sequences, and public Q&A reframing.
Lower-elementary kana routines now include confusable-shape sorting, small-kana placement, mora-to-kana self-checks, homophone context checks, and romaji-input repair.
Early handwriting routines now include katakana label copying, sentence-copy self-checks, clean rewriting after feedback, and dialogue line-break copying.
Lower-elementary information use now includes picture dictionary searching, gojuon lookup practice, source-note boundaries, fact/opinion tagging, and quotation-summary boundary control.
Lower-elementary writing now deepens sentence expansion, caption-detail sentences, oral paragraph rehearsal, opinion-reason-example chains, evidence-tagged summaries, and audience-hook revision.
Upper-elementary kanji work now includes morpheme field webs, compound paraphrase, homophone reason notes, abstract noun maps, exception notes, and public-text register analysis.
Upper-elementary inquiry now covers source reliability checks, multi-source note sorting, source claim-evidence matrices, portfolio source logs, and media-message intent analysis.

Upper-elementary oral work now includes peer evidence questions, building on evidence, visual source presentation, discussion facilitation, debate summary listening, and evidence-based presentation Q&A.
Upper-elementary literary reading now includes motif trackers, viewpoint quote notes, poem line-break effects, theme-response revision, perspective-shift analysis, and cross-genre theme comparison.

Lower-secondary informational reading now deepens multi-source gist comparison, warrant diagrams, counterclaim evidence rating, media framing synthesis, and N2-like stance and implication reading for native school argument.

Lower-secondary writing now develops quote-paraphrase boundaries, evidence-commentary revision, poem image essays, thesis scope, counterargument-rebuttal chains, citation commentary, and public issue editorials.

Lower-secondary source work now includes reliability reason notes, headline evidence checks, source bias-purpose matrices, infographic data audits, evidence hierarchy matrices, and source-gap question refinement.
Lower-secondary oral argument now includes seminar question-to-claim moves, source-card explanations, debate case outlines, evidence-return facilitation, and cross-examination question design.

Lower-secondary classical and language culture work now includes recitation notes, classical-modern honorific register comparison, kobun auxiliary cards, kanbun readback checks, and classical-modern allusion response.

Upper-secondary information use now adds source genre reliability heuristics, evidence-tagged inquiry notes, media framing grids, synthesis-matrix-to-thesis conversion, bibliography annotation audits, and publication transparency notes.
Upper-secondary informational reading now includes data-claim and graph rhetoric, infographic source-note reading, causal evidence chains, and analogy argument testing.

Upper-secondary writing now develops public issue explainers, comparative news summaries, literature-review gap statements, counterargument revision, capstone question narrowing, feasible recommendations, and public abstracts.
Upper-secondary literary reading now deepens setting as social lens, critical lens limitations, narrator reliability tests, poetic diction patterns, and comparative literary-cultural introductions.

Upper-secondary language culture now connects present-day classical quotation, classical auxiliary disambiguation, kanbun allusion tracing, reception-history caveats, translation notes, and language-identity case comparison.
Upper-secondary speaking work now adds inquiry interview design, evidence-slide briefings, seminar response synthesis, slide evidence density control, and equity-aware symposium moderation.
Lower-elementary oral language now links echo reading, retell connectors, instruction-listening checks, interview main-point notes, and listening-note summaries.
Lower-elementary informational reading now moves from picture sequence questions and survey graph sentences to diagram-label claim support and visual-text synthesis sentences.
Lower-elementary discussion now grows from pair response stems to evidence roles, balanced presentation boards, facilitator return questions, and peer revision conference stems.
Lower-elementary reflection now includes story enjoyment reasons, kind partner feedback, one-target paragraph revision, strategy-switch notes, and inquiry source-gap reflection.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Discussion & Presentation now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Kanji & Morphographic Literacy now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.

Project-authored spiral expansion: Grammar & Language Awareness now has recurring grade 1-12 micro-topics that deepen a teachable routine each year without turning individual kanji or vocabulary items into graph nodes.
